article_title: Responsible authorities
obligation_text_short: Each State Party shall designate or establish a National IHR Focal Point (NFP) accessible at all times and notify WHO of its designation and contact details.
anchoring_reason: Requires establishment or formal designation of a national authority with defined functions, mandate, and 24/7 operational capacity; functions cannot be fulfilled through informal administrative arrangements
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal or regulatory instrument designating the NFP; definition of NFP functions and powers; authority to communicate with WHO and coordinate across government sectors; 24/7 operational mandate
implementation_domain: CC2 – Coordination and NFP communications; CC1 – National legislation
rights_implications: No direct rights implications; NFP is the gateway for all subsequent IHR obligations whose activation may affect rights
federalism_implications: Yes – in federal systems, NFP must coordinate with subnational health authorities; designation must be accompanied by formal inter-governmental coordination arrangements; competence for health may rest at subnational level
notes: CC Workbook CC2 requires NFP to have been established by 2007 with expanded roles (risk assessment, advocacy) requiring institutional backing; CC1 explicitly notes that a sufficient legal framework was required from the date IHR entered into force in 2007; WHO recommends legal basis for NFP functions

article_title: Surveillance
obligation_text_short: Each State Party shall develop, strengthen and maintain, as soon as possible but no later than five years from entry into force, the capacity to detect, assess, notify and report events as specified in Annex 1.
anchoring_reason: Requires institutional structures, designated surveillance units, multi-level reporting chains, case definitions, thresholds, and recurrent operational capacity at all administrative levels (local, intermediate, national)
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legislative or regulatory framework mandating a national surveillance system; designated units at local, intermediate, and national levels; legal reporting obligations from community to national level; resource allocation; case definitions for priority diseases including Annex 2 diseases
implementation_domain: CC1 – National legislation; CC3 – Surveillance; CC5 – Preparedness
rights_implications: Partial – population-level surveillance may implicate privacy and data protection rights (see Art. 45); community-level reporting may involve sensitive personal health information
federalism_implications: Yes – Annex 1A requires capacities at all administrative levels; in federal systems, subnational entities must be legally empowered and adequately resourced; community-level surveillance may fall under subnational or municipal jurisdiction
notes: CC Workbook CC3 requires both indicator-based and event-based surveillance; Art. 5(2) allows extensions with justified implementation plan; Annex 1A par.2 requires prior self-assessment; CC3 specifies timely reporting from at least 80% of reporting units as a Level 2 indicator

article_title: Surveillance
obligation_text_short: A State Party may obtain an extension of up to two plus two years by reporting to WHO with a justified need and an implementation plan; after the extension period the State Party shall report annually on progress made towards full implementation.
anchoring_reason: The extension request is procedurally international-facing; however, the underlying implementation plan implies domestic regulatory and institutional actions whose adequacy WHO will assess; annual reporting implies a domestic monitoring mechanism
minimum_domestic_requirement: Formalized national IHR implementation plan adopted by competent authority; annual progress reporting mechanism; designated authority responsible for reporting to WHO; link to CC1 assessment process
implementation_domain: CC1 – National legislation, policy and financing; CC5 – Preparedness
rights_implications: No direct rights implications
federalism_implications: Partial – in federal systems, implementation plans must account for subnational jurisdiction and resource distribution; federal government typically responsible for WHO reporting even when implementation is subnational
notes: Extension request itself does not require new legislation but the associated implementation plan implies legislative and institutional commitments; WHO may provide assistance per Art. 5(3); closely linked to Annex 1A par.2 self-assessment

article_title: Notification
obligation_text_short: Each State Party shall assess events using the Annex 2 decision instrument and notify WHO through the NFP within 24 hours of assessment of any event that may constitute a PHEIC, and of any health measures implemented in response.
anchoring_reason: Requires a legally empowered NFP with the mandate to collect, assess, and transmit public health information within 24 hours; implies a functioning risk assessment system and mandatory upward reporting from subnational to national level
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal mandate for NFP to notify WHO; legal obligation on health authorities at all levels to report to the NFP; use of Annex 2 decision instrument embedded in national law or standard operating procedures; defined risk assessment authority
rights_implications: Partial – data reported may include personal health information; Art. 45 data protection obligations apply to information transmitted to WHO
federalism_implications: Yes – in federal systems, subnational health authorities must have a legal obligation to report upward to the NFP within the 24-hour window; vertical reporting chains require explicit legal or regulatory basis
notes: CC Workbook CC3 (Level 1) requires 100% of Annex 2-qualifying events to be notified to WHO; response to WHO verification requests within 24h also required (Art. 10); closely linked to Art. 7 (unusual events) and Annex 2 decision instrument

article_title: Notification
obligation_text_short: Following notification, the State Party shall continue communicating to WHO timely, accurate and sufficiently detailed public health information on the notified event, including case definitions, laboratory results, conditions affecting spread, and health measures employed; and shall report difficulties faced and support needed.
anchoring_reason: Requires ongoing information collection and reporting systems; may function through existing administrative NFP capacity but requires functional cross-sectoral reporting chains and data aggregation capacity that may need legal grounding
minimum_domestic_requirement: Standard operating procedures for ongoing event monitoring and international reporting; NFP with legal authority to collect information from all relevant sectors; data sharing protocols between health, laboratory, and other sectors
implementation_domain: CC2 – NFP; CC3 – Surveillance; CC6 – Risk communication
rights_implications: Partial – detailed case-level information may implicate personal data protection (Art. 45); individual case data must be anonymized or handled per national law
federalism_implications: Partial – in federal systems, ongoing information flow from subnational authorities through national NFP to WHO must be legally mandated and operationally maintained for the duration of the event
notes: CC Workbook CC3 (Level 2) specifies NFP response to 100% of WHO verification requests within 24h; this is a continuous obligation throughout the event lifecycle; laboratory confirmation capacity (CC8) directly feeds this obligation

article_title: Information-sharing during unexpected or unusual public health events
obligation_text_short: If a State Party has evidence of an unexpected or unusual public health event within its territory, irrespective of origin or source, which may constitute a PHEIC, it shall provide WHO with all relevant public health information; Art. 6 provisions apply in full.
anchoring_reason: Requires event detection and information sharing across sectors including non-health sectors (chemical, radiological, biological); cross-sectoral legal reporting mandates are necessary because the trigger is source-neutral (natural, accidental, deliberate)
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal mandate for NFP to receive and transmit information from all relevant sectors; cross-sectoral reporting obligations not limited to the health sector; event-based surveillance system with multi-source capture capacity
rights_implications: Partial – information reported may include personal health data; Art. 45 applies; CBRN events may involve classified or sensitive national security information
federalism_implications: Yes – cross-sectoral reporting must reach the NFP from subnational levels across all sector ministries; in federal systems, cross-sectoral mandates may not uniformly apply at all government levels
notes: US understanding on file confirms this covers natural, accidental, or deliberate release of CBRN materials; CC Workbook CC3 notes event-based surveillance must capture information from multiple non-health sources including radiation protection offices, customs, military, media; directly cross-references Annex 2 decision instrument

article_title: Consultation
obligation_text_short: In the case of events not requiring notification, a State Party may keep WHO advised through the NFP and consult WHO on appropriate health measures; such communications are treated as confidential by WHO.
legal_force: Binding (as a permission; presupposes functional NFP capacity)
anchoring_reason: Requires a functional NFP with authority and capacity to consult WHO on sub-threshold events; may operate through existing administrative authority but requires assessment, coordination, and communication capacity that may need legal grounding
minimum_domestic_requirement: Functional NFP with designated communication authority; standard operating procedures for below-threshold events; assessment capacity using Annex 2 instrument for events that do not clearly meet notification criteria
rights_implications: No direct rights implications at consultation stage; subsequent measures may implicate rights
federalism_implications: Partial – NFP must be able to gather subnational information on sub-threshold events for consultations
notes: This is a permission rather than a hard obligation but presupposes functional assessment and coordination capacity; CC Workbook notes NFP should proactively engage WHO; WHO assistance for assessment is available per Art. 8 last sentence

article_title: Other reports
obligation_text_short: States Parties shall, as far as practicable, inform WHO within 24 hours of evidence of a public health risk identified outside their territory that may cause international disease spread, as manifested by exported or imported human cases, vectors, or contaminated goods.
anchoring_reason: Requires surveillance and monitoring systems that extend to travelers, vectors, and goods entering from abroad; NFP must have authority to collect and transmit cross-border information; 'as far as practicable' qualification limits the absolute obligation
minimum_domestic_requirement: Border surveillance systems; mechanisms for monitoring imported cases, vectors, and contaminated goods; designated authority for cross-border risk reporting; NFP reporting capacity for external risks
rights_implications: No direct rights implications
federalism_implications: Partial – in federal systems with decentralized border control, subnational authorities at PoE may hold relevant information; national coordination mechanisms needed to consolidate and report to WHO within 24 hours
notes: Qualified by 'as far as practicable'; uncertainty remains on what threshold of evidence triggers this obligation; CC Workbook CC3 (Level 3) notes arrangements with neighbouring countries for surveillance data sharing as an additional achievement indicator

article_title: Verification
obligation_text_short: When WHO requests verification, each State Party shall provide: within 24 hours, an initial reply or acknowledgement; within 24 hours, available public health information on the status of the events concerned; and information relevant for risk assessment under Art. 6.
anchoring_reason: Requires NFP with legal authority to gather information from all public health actors within a 24-hour window; requires inter-sectoral reporting chains and formal information-sharing agreements between health, laboratory, and other relevant authorities
minimum_domestic_requirement: NFP with legal information-gathering powers across sectors; defined response protocols for WHO verification requests; inter-authority data sharing mechanisms; 24/7 NFP operational capacity
rights_implications: Partial – information gathering may touch on personal health data; Art. 45 applies
federalism_implications: Yes – in federal systems, the national NFP must have legal authority to obtain information from subnational authorities and report to WHO within 24 hours; vertical information-gathering powers must be formally established
notes: CC Workbook CC3 (Level 1) sets 100% response to WHO verification requests within 24h; failure to respond may lead WHO to share available information with other States Parties per Art. 10(4); closely linked to Art. 6 assessment procedures and Annex 2

article_title: Public health response
obligation_text_short: Each State Party shall develop, strengthen and maintain, as soon as possible but no later than five years from entry into force, the capacity to respond promptly and effectively to public health risks and PHEICs as set out in Annex 1.
anchoring_reason: Requires legal basis for public health emergency response powers; multidisciplinary rapid response teams; infection prevention and control frameworks; case management authority; national emergency plans; laboratory diagnostic capacity; decontamination capacity
minimum_domestic_requirement: Public health emergency legislation granting response powers; legal designation of response authority; mandate for multidisciplinary rapid response teams (RRTs); national emergency response plan with legal status; infection prevention and control (IPC) regulatory framework; laboratory diagnostic capacity or agreements with WHO collaborating centres
implementation_domain: CC4 – Response; CC5 – Preparedness; CC1 – National legislation; CC8 – Laboratory
rights_implications: Yes – response measures including isolation, quarantine, and compulsory treatment have direct implications for liberty, bodily integrity, privacy, and non-discrimination; safeguards under Arts. 31, 32, 42, and 45 must be domestically anchored
federalism_implications: Yes – response capacity must be operational at local, intermediate, and national levels; inter-governmental coordination is essential; subnational authorities often hold primary responsibility for hospital-level response
notes: CC Workbook CC4 specifies rapid response capacity, case management, infection control, decontamination, and antimicrobial resistance monitoring; Art. 13(2) mirrors Art. 5(2) extension mechanism; Art. 13(5) creates a qualified obligation on other States to support WHO-coordinated response

article_title: Core capacity requirements — self-assessment and action planning
obligation_text_short: Each State Party shall assess, within two years of entry into force for that State, the ability of existing national structures and resources to meet minimum core capacity requirements and shall develop and implement plans of action to ensure these capacities are present and functioning.
anchoring_reason: The assessment and plan of action must translate into institutional mandates and resource allocation decisions; a plan without formal government commitment and legal basis for implementation lacks binding domestic effect
minimum_domestic_requirement: Formal self-assessment mechanism designated to a responsible authority; national IHR action plan with timelines and milestones adopted by competent government body; designation of responsible authorities per level; financing commitment; legal review of existing national structures
implementation_domain: CC1 – National legislation, policy and financing; CC5 – Preparedness
rights_implications: No direct rights implications
federalism_implications: Yes – in federal systems, self-assessment must include subnational structures, capacities, and legal frameworks; the plan must assign responsibilities across levels of government
notes: CC Workbook CC1 (Level less than 1/1) notes that assessment of legislation relative to IHR implementation is strongly urged by the World Health Assembly; recommended in WHO Toolkit for IHR Implementation in National Legislation; CC1 (Level 2) requires recommendations from assessment to be implemented

article_title: Core capacity — community and primary public health response level
obligation_text_short: At community and primary public health response levels: capacity to detect events involving disease or death above expected levels; to report all essential information immediately to the appropriate level; and to implement preliminary control measures immediately.
anchoring_reason: Requires designated local health authorities with formal reporting obligations, defined case definitions, population thresholds for alert and action, and legal authority to implement preliminary control measures without awaiting higher-level authorization
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal mandate for community health units; reporting obligation to intermediate or national level; defined case definitions and alert thresholds; authority to implement preliminary containment measures; financing for community-level surveillance
rights_implications: Partial – preliminary control measures may restrict movement or require medical intervention; must be proportionate and respect Art. 32 dignity standards
federalism_implications: Yes – community-level capacities are typically under subnational or municipal jurisdiction; legal empowerment at these levels is essential; central government may need to devolve powers with accompanying resources
notes: CC Workbook CC3 requires baseline estimates, trends, and thresholds for alert and action to be defined at community/primary response level (Level 1); deviation detection and action at primary response level is a Level 2 indicator; strong federalism implication in countries where health is a subnational constitutional competence

article_title: Core capacity — intermediate public health response level
obligation_text_short: At the intermediate public health response level: capacity to confirm the status of reported events and support or implement additional control measures; and to assess reported events immediately and, if urgent, report all essential information to the national level.
anchoring_reason: Requires designated intermediate health authorities with assessment, reporting, and response authority; implies formal mandate, adequate staffing, and resource allocation at the intermediate level; legal basis for escalation to national level must be clear
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal designation of intermediate-level health authorities; authority to assess and escalate urgent events; standard operating procedures for urgent event escalation to national level; resource allocation for intermediate-level response capacity
rights_implications: Partial – control measures implemented at intermediate level may restrict movement or mandate treatment
federalism_implications: Yes – in federal systems, the intermediate level often corresponds to state or provincial authorities; requires clear inter-governmental reporting chains and defined escalation criteria
notes: CC Workbook defines urgency criteria as 'serious public health impact and/or unusual or unexpected nature with high potential for spread'; the intermediate level may not exist as a distinct administrative tier in all national systems; uncertainty on operationalization in highly centralized or highly decentralized systems

article_title: Core capacity — national level assessment and notification
obligation_text_short: At the national level: capacity to assess all reports of urgent events within 48 hours; to notify WHO immediately through the NFP when assessment indicates a notifiable event per Art. 6 and Annex 2; and to inform WHO as required under Art. 7 and Art. 9(2).
anchoring_reason: Requires a functional, legally mandated national assessment body with risk assessment capacity, decision-making authority on notifiability, and 24/7 operational capability; use of the Annex 2 decision instrument must be embedded in national procedure or law
minimum_domestic_requirement: National authority with legal power to receive, assess, and transmit public health notifications to WHO; designated risk assessment unit; legal or regulatory incorporation of Annex 2 decision instrument; 24/7 NFP operational mandate; defined criteria for 'urgent events'
implementation_domain: CC2 – NFP; CC3 – Surveillance; CC4 – Response
rights_implications: No direct rights implications at assessment and notification stage; subsequent response measures may implicate rights
federalism_implications: Yes – information flows from all subnational levels to the national NFP must be legally mandated and operationally functional; 48-hour assessment window requires real-time upward reporting
notes: CC Workbook CC3 (Level 2) specifies that all reports of urgent events be assessed within 48 hours; NFP must respond to 100% of WHO verification requests within 24 hours (CC3 Level 1); strong legal anchoring required for 24/7 NFP operational capacity; links to CC8 (laboratory confirmation)

article_title: Core capacity — national level public health response
obligation_text_short: At the national level: capacity to determine control measures; provide specialized staff, laboratory analysis, and logistical support; conduct on-site assistance; link directly with senior health officials; liaise with other government ministries; establish a national public health emergency response plan with multidisciplinary/multisectoral teams; and operate on a 24-hour basis.
anchoring_reason: Requires statutory public health emergency powers; multi-ministerial coordination mechanisms; legal basis for a national emergency response plan; authority to deploy multidisciplinary rapid response teams on a 24/7 basis; legal basis for inter-ministerial liaison; laboratory diagnostic authority or WHO collaborating centre agreements
minimum_domestic_requirement: Public health emergency legislation; national emergency response plan with legal status; designated multisectoral coordinating mechanism with formal mandate; inter-ministerial coordination obligation; laboratory diagnostic capacity or formal agreements with WHO collaborating centres; legal authority for 24/7 rapid response team deployment
implementation_domain: CC4 – Response; CC5 – Preparedness; CC2 – Coordination; CC8 – Laboratory
rights_implications: Yes – emergency powers and multisectoral response directly implicate rights to liberty, privacy, and non-discrimination; the national plan must include rights safeguards consistent with Arts. 31, 32, 42, and 45
federalism_implications: Yes – national emergency plans must cover the full territory and be operationally linked to subnational response structures; in federal systems, inter-governmental emergency response protocols require formal agreements or constitutional arrangements
notes: CC Workbook CC4 specifies: RRTs deployable within 48 hours; national IPC policy; antimicrobial resistance monitoring; coordination with PoE; laboratory capacity or agreements with collaborating centres; command and control procedures; CC2 requires annual stakeholder updates on IHR implementation

article_title: Core capacity — designated PoE baseline (at all times)
obligation_text_short: At all times, designated airports, ports, and ground crossings shall have capacity to: provide access to appropriate medical services and diagnostic facilities; provide transport for ill travellers; provide trained personnel for conveyance inspection; ensure a safe environment at PoE facilities; and provide vector and reservoir control programmes.
anchoring_reason: Requires designated competent authorities at each PoE with legal mandate, trained staff, equipment, and infrastructure; implies regulatory requirements for conveyance inspection, sanitation standards, and vector control programmes applicable at PoE
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal designation of competent authority at each designated PoE; staffing, equipment, and diagnostic facility standards; sanitation and vector control regulatory requirements; medical referral arrangements; trained inspector workforce
implementation_domain: PoE – Points of entry; CC4 – Response
rights_implications: Partial – inspection of travellers and conveyances implicates privacy and dignity rights; must comply with Art. 32 (dignity) and Art. 42 (non-discrimination)
federalism_implications: Yes – in federal systems, PoE may fall under different governmental jurisdictions; coordination and legal clarity on jurisdiction and applicable standards is essential
notes: CC Workbook PoE section specifies distinct indicators for airports, seaports, and ground crossings; 'at all times' requirement means this is not only an emergency-triggered capacity; baseline infrastructure investment requires sustained financing (CC1)

article_title: Core capacity — designated PoE PHEIC response
obligation_text_short: For events that may constitute a PHEIC, designated PoE shall have capacity to: establish and maintain a public health emergency contingency plan with a coordinator; arrange assessment and care of affected travellers; provide separate interview space; apply quarantine; apply decontamination measures; apply entry/exit controls; and access specialized equipment for potentially infectious travellers.
anchoring_reason: Requires legal authority to apply quarantine, isolation, entry/exit controls, and decontamination at PoE; contingency plans must have formal legal status and be operationally tested; coordinator must have formal legal authority and defined powers
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal powers for quarantine and isolation at PoE; entry and exit health screening authority; designated PoE emergency coordinator with legal mandate; formally adopted and tested contingency plans; specialized equipment and trained personnel for potentially infectious traveller management
rights_implications: Yes – quarantine, isolation, and entry/exit controls directly implicate the right to liberty and freedom of movement; safeguards under Arts. 31(1), 32, and 42 must apply at PoE level; the principle of least restrictive measure applies
federalism_implications: Yes – PoE authority often spans multiple jurisdictions; inter-governmental agreements on health measures at points of entry are critical for consistent rights-compliant application
notes: Closely linked to Arts. 20, 21, 22; CC Workbook PoE indicators include functional contingency plans, isolation facilities, quarantine authority, and decontamination capability; rights safeguards from Arts. 31-32 apply to all measures implemented at PoE level

article_title: General obligations at points of entry
obligation_text_short: Each State Party shall ensure Annex 1 capacities for designated PoE are developed within the Art. 5/13 timeframe; identify the competent authority at each designated PoE; and furnish relevant data on sources of infection or contamination at PoE upon WHO request.
anchoring_reason: Requires a legal instrument formally designating PoE and individually identifying the competent authority at each; the identification is specific (per PoE) not merely general; WHO data-sharing obligation requires an administrative reporting mechanism
minimum_domestic_requirement: Regulatory instrument designating each PoE; formal identification of competent authority at each designated PoE; inter-authority communication protocols; WHO reporting mechanism for PoE-related data; linkage of PoE authorities with national NFP
implementation_domain: PoE; CC2 – Coordination; CC1 – National legislation
rights_implications: No direct rights implications at the designation level; competent authority's subsequent actions implicate rights
federalism_implications: Yes – PoE competent authorities may be under different levels of government; formal designation must clarify jurisdiction and chain of command between national and subnational authorities
notes: Art. 19(b) requires identification of competent authority at each designated PoE specifically — not merely a generic national PoE authority; uncertainty on how to handle informal or ad hoc crossings not formally designated but through which significant traffic occurs

article_title: Airports and ports — designation
obligation_text_short: States Parties shall designate the airports and ports that shall develop the core capacity requirements provided in Annex 1.
anchoring_reason: Designation requires a formal administrative or regulatory act specifying which airports and ports carry IHR obligations; the designation informs resource allocation, competent authority identification, and the legal obligations of port/airport operators
minimum_domestic_requirement: Formal list of designated airports and ports adopted by competent authority; regulatory status of the designation; notification to WHO per Art. 20(3); designation criteria accounting for volume, frequency, and public health risk
implementation_domain: PoE; CC1 – Legislation, policy and financing
rights_implications: No direct rights implications at the designation level
federalism_implications: Partial – airports and ports may be under national, subnational, or private authority; legal clarity on which entity is the 'competent authority' for IHR purposes at each location is required
notes: CC Workbook PoE indicators distinguish between airports, seaports, and ground crossings with distinct capacity profiles; WHO may certify airports and ports per Art. 20(4) — optional but signals compliance; uncertainty on minimum threshold of traffic volume that triggers the obligation to designate

article_title: Airports and ports — Ship Sanitation Certificates
obligation_text_short: States Parties shall ensure Ship Sanitation Control Exemption Certificates and Ship Sanitation Control Certificates are issued in accordance with Art. 39 and the model provided in Annex 3.
anchoring_reason: Requires a legally empowered port authority with inspection and certification authority; domestic regulatory framework for ship sanitation inspection must conform to Art. 39 and Annex 3 model format; issuance is a legal act with international effects
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal authority for ship sanitation inspections at designated ports; designation of authority to issue Annex 3-conforming certificates; trained inspectors; enforcement powers if inspection reveals deficiencies; procedure for certificate validity and extension per Art. 39
rights_implications: No direct rights implications
federalism_implications: Partial – port authority jurisdiction may vary between national and subnational levels; the 'competent authority' for certification must be clearly legally designated
notes: Certificates must conform to Annex 3 model format; validity is 6 months with a possible 1-month extension per Art. 39; Art. 20(2) must be read together with Art. 39; ports must be on the WHO-authorized list per Art. 20(3)

article_title: Airports and ports — notification of authorized ports to WHO
obligation_text_short: Each State Party shall send WHO a list of ports authorized to issue Ship Sanitation Certificates and inform WHO of any changes; WHO publishes this information.
anchoring_reason: Requires an administrative procedure to maintain and update the list of authorized ports; may be achieved through existing administrative NFP or port authority capacity without requiring new legislation, but requires a designated responsible authority
minimum_domestic_requirement: Designated authority for WHO communications on port authorization; procedure for updating the list upon changes to port authorization status; linkage with the NFP for WHO communications
rights_implications: No direct rights implications
federalism_implications: No significant federalism implications — port authorization is typically a national administrative function
notes: Primarily an administrative and procedural obligation; WHO publishes the resulting list publicly; uncertainty on the process for revoking or modifying the authorized status of a port

article_title: Ground crossings — designation and bilateral agreements
obligation_text_short: A State Party may designate ground crossings that shall develop Annex 1 capacities; States Parties sharing common borders should consider entering into bilateral or multilateral agreements and jointly designating adjacent ground crossings.
legal_force: Binding for designation process; soft obligation ('should consider') for bilateral agreements
anchoring_reason: Formal designation requires an administrative act; bilateral health agreements typically require legislative authorization and/or ratification; public health criteria for designation must be legally defined
minimum_domestic_requirement: Criteria for ground crossing designation based on public health risk and traffic volume; domestic legal authority for bilateral health agreements at ground crossings; joint designation mechanism; compliance of designated crossings with Annex 1B capacity requirements
rights_implications: No direct rights implications at designation level; health measures subsequently applied at crossings implicate rights
federalism_implications: Yes – ground crossings are frequently located within subnational territories; authority over them may be constitutionally shared between national and subnational governments; bilateral agreements may require subnational participation in federal systems
notes: 'Should consider' is a soft obligation; CC Workbook CC3 (Level 3) lists arrangements with neighbouring countries to share surveillance data; uncertainty on what triggers the obligation to designate a crossing versus permitting state discretion

article_title: Role of competent authorities at PoE
obligation_text_short: Competent authorities shall: monitor arrivals and departures for contamination; maintain sanitary conditions at PoE facilities; supervise decontamination of conveyances and goods; advise conveyance operators of control measures; safely dispose of contaminated materials; control ship sewage discharge; supervise service providers; maintain effective contingency arrangements; and communicate with the NFP.
anchoring_reason: Each listed function requires a legal mandate and sufficient authority to act; health, environmental, transport, and maritime regulatory powers must be legally integrated; the contingency arrangement obligation (Art. 22(1)(h)) implies a planning duty; NFP communication (Art. 22(1)(i)) must be institutionally operationalized
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal empowerment of competent authorities at PoE across regulatory domains (health, environment, transport, maritime); inter-authority coordination protocols with the NFP; contingency planning obligation with formal status; authority over service providers at PoE; enforcement powers for sanitation and decontamination
rights_implications: Partial – inspection and decontamination powers may implicate property rights and dignity; traveller-facing measures must comply with Arts. 32 and 42
federalism_implications: Yes – PoE competent authorities may span national, subnational, and municipal jurisdictions; customs, health, transport, and maritime powers are often distributed across levels; clear jurisdictional assignment is essential for the integrated functions required by Art. 22(1)
notes: Art. 22(1)(h) requires 'effective contingency arrangements' implying tested and operationally capable arrangements; Art. 22(1)(i) requires direct NFP communication which must be institutionalized; CC Workbook PoE indicators confirm these as distinct measurable capacities

article_title: Health measures on arrival and departure — informed consent
obligation_text_short: No medical examination, vaccination, prophylaxis, or health measure under these Regulations shall be carried out on travellers without their prior express informed consent or that of their parents or guardians, except as provided in Art. 31(2) (imminent public health risk).
anchoring_reason: Informed consent for medical interventions imposed under public health authority requires a domestic legal basis; exceptions to consent (Art. 31(2)) must also be legally defined with clear criteria and procedural safeguards; parental/guardian consent provisions must be operationally established
minimum_domestic_requirement: Domestic law recognizing the informed consent requirement for public health interventions on travellers; defined and narrowly bounded exceptions with procedural safeguards; parental and guardian consent provisions; compatible with 'national law and international obligations of the State Party' per Art. 23(3)
rights_implications: Yes – core right to bodily integrity and personal autonomy; must be balanced against public health necessity under Art. 31(2); links to Art. 32 (dignity) and Art. 31 (entry conditions)
federalism_implications: Partial – informed consent standards may differ across subnational jurisdictions; a national floor is required to ensure IHR conformity
notes: Exceptions under Art. 31(2) require imminent public health risk and least invasive measures; the provision explicitly conditions application on conformity with 'national law and international obligations of the State Party'; uncertainty on whether all existing national health laws fully satisfy IHR-mandated informed consent standards

article_title: Health measures on arrival and departure — vaccination risk information
obligation_text_short: Travellers to be vaccinated or offered prophylaxis pursuant to these Regulations, or their parents or guardians, shall be informed of any risk associated with vaccination or non-vaccination; States Parties shall inform medical practitioners of these requirements in accordance with national law.
anchoring_reason: Requires a domestic mechanism for communicating vaccination risk information to travellers and to medical practitioners; implies regulatory guidance for health professionals at PoE and in travel medicine settings; information must be provided 'in accordance with national law'
minimum_domestic_requirement: Regulatory guidance or national standards on informed consent for travel vaccinations; obligation on health authorities to disseminate requirements to medical practitioners; information materials for travellers in accessible language; conformity with 'law and international obligations of the State Party' per Art. 23(4)
rights_implications: Yes – right to information as a precondition for genuine informed consent; right to health decision-making; links to Art. 23(3) and Art. 32
federalism_implications: Partial – practitioner regulation may be a subnational competence in federal systems; national standards must be established and communicated to subnational practitioner bodies
notes: 'In accordance with the law and international obligations of the State Party' implies a national floor of protection; uncertainty on how to operationalize in systems where travel vaccinations are primarily administered through private health providers not subject to direct governmental instruction

article_title: Conveyance operators — obligations
obligation_text_short: States Parties shall take all practicable measures to ensure conveyance operators comply with WHO-recommended health measures; inform travellers of applicable health measures; and permanently keep conveyances free of sources of infection or contamination, including vectors and reservoirs.
anchoring_reason: Imposing obligations on private operators (airlines, shipping companies, rail and road operators) requires domestic regulatory authority; compliance enforcement requires a legal mandate; traveller information requirements must be operationally specified in domestic regulation
minimum_domestic_requirement: Domestic regulations imposing IHR-related obligations on conveyance operators; enforcement authority with inspection and sanction powers; information dissemination requirements to travellers; reference to WHO-recommended health measures in domestic operator regulatory framework
rights_implications: Partial – traveller information rights; privacy concerns in health measure communication
federalism_implications: Partial – conveyance operator regulation is typically national; however, subnational authorities may have concurrent or complementary regulatory powers
notes: 'All practicable measures' qualifier limits the absolute scope; Annex 4 provides specific provisions for conveyances; Annex 5 addresses vector-borne disease measures on conveyances; CC Workbook links this to PoE and CC4 response capacities

article_title: Affected conveyances — health measures
obligation_text_short: If clinical signs or evidence of a public health risk are found on board a conveyance, the competent authority may disinfect, decontaminate, disinsect, or derat the conveyance; may isolate it as necessary; and shall report additional measures to the NFP.
anchoring_reason: Competent authority must have legal power to order and supervise decontamination and isolation of conveyances; the NFP reporting requirement must be institutionally mandated; procedures must be aligned with WHO-recommended methods per Art. 27(1)(b)
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal authority for conveyance inspection, decontamination, and isolation at designated PoE; reporting obligation to NFP upon applying additional measures; procedures specifying use of WHO-recommended methods; unambiguous delegation of authority to competent authority at each PoE
rights_implications: Partial – isolation of a conveyance may restrict the liberty of persons on board; must be proportionate and comply with Art. 32; Art. 27(3) specifies conditions for lifting affected status
federalism_implications: Partial – competent authority at the relevant PoE must have clear and unambiguous jurisdictional authority; in federal systems, clarity on which government level exercises these powers is essential
notes: Art. 27(2) provides an alternative where the competent authority lacks capacity — notification to the next PoE is required; uncertainty on how rapidly these emergency powers can be legally invoked without advance warning procedures

article_title: Travellers under public health observation
obligation_text_short: A suspect traveller placed under public health observation on arrival may continue an international voyage if not posing an imminent public health risk; the State Party shall inform the destination competent authority; the traveller shall report to that authority on arrival.
anchoring_reason: Requires domestic authority to place travellers under non-custodial public health observation; inter-PoE notification mechanism between national authorities; legal definition of 'suspect traveller' and 'imminent public health risk'; legal basis for the traveller's duty to report at destination
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal authority for placing travellers under non-custodial public health observation; defined criteria for 'suspect traveller' and 'imminent public health risk'; inter-PoE and inter-State notification procedures; mechanism for imposing and monitoring traveller's reporting obligation at destination; conformity with Art. 32 dignity standards
rights_implications: Yes – liberty and freedom of movement implicated; public health observation is a partial restriction; proportionality required; must be consistent with Arts. 32 and 42
federalism_implications: Partial – notification to the destination PoE may cross subnational jurisdictions; coordination between state/provincial health authorities may be needed to receive and monitor arriving travellers under observation
notes: Public health observation is less restrictive than quarantine; the mechanism presupposes inter-State or inter-PoE communication capacity; uncertainty on enforcement mechanism for traveller's obligation to report at destination in the absence of an international compliance mechanism; Art. 30 begins with 'subject to Art. 43'

article_title: Health measures relating to entry — prohibition on invasive entry conditions
obligation_text_short: Invasive medical examination, vaccination, or other prophylaxis shall not be required as a condition of entry of any traveller, except where: (a) necessary to determine a public health risk; (b) as a condition for travellers seeking temporary or permanent residence; (c) per Art. 43 or Annexes 6-7; or (d) per Art. 23 procedures.
anchoring_reason: Requires domestic law to incorporate this prohibition; existing entry requirements must fall within the permitted exceptions; immigration and health laws must be reviewed for IHR compatibility; domestic law must define the permitted exceptions narrowly
minimum_domestic_requirement: Domestic law prohibiting invasive entry requirements except in permitted circumstances; narrowly defined legal exceptions corresponding to Art. 31(1)(a)-(d); comprehensive review of existing border health entry measures for IHR compatibility; available legal challenge mechanism for travellers denied entry
rights_implications: Yes – right to entry; right against invasive medical procedures as a condition of movement; non-discrimination in entry; directly implicates freedom of movement and bodily integrity; links to Art. 42 (non-discriminatory implementation)
federalism_implications: Partial – entry requirements are typically national; however, health standards applied at subnational PoE must conform to the national legal framework which must itself comply with Art. 31(1)
notes: The four exceptions are cumulative and alternative (Art. 31(1)(a)-(d)); tension with Art. 43 (additional measures) must be managed through proportionality and scientific basis requirements; CC Workbook does not directly assess compliance with this prohibition; tension may arise between IHR Art. 31 and domestic immigration law

article_title: Health measures relating to entry — compulsory measures in exceptional circumstances
obligation_text_short: If a traveller refuses examination or documentation requirements and there is evidence of an imminent public health risk, the State Party may, in accordance with national law, compel the least invasive examination, vaccination, or other established health measures including isolation, quarantine, or public health observation.
legal_force: Binding (conditional on 'national law' and 'imminent public health risk')
anchoring_reason: Compulsory public health interventions on travellers must be grounded in national law; the principle of 'least invasive' means must be legally established; criteria for 'imminent public health risk' must be legally defined; judicial or administrative oversight is implied
minimum_domestic_requirement: National law authorizing compulsory health measures with strict and defined criteria (imminent risk); explicit legal requirement to use least restrictive and invasive means; procedural safeguards including review and appeal rights; compatibility with Arts. 32, 42, and 45; conformity with ICCPR Art. 9 and equivalent instruments
rights_implications: Yes – significant rights implications: right to liberty, bodily integrity, freedom of movement; compulsory medical interventions are among the most rights-sensitive public health measures; safeguards required under Art. 32 (dignity), Art. 42 (non-discrimination), and Art. 45 (data protection)
federalism_implications: Partial – in federal systems, compulsory public health powers may reside at different government levels; a national minimum standard of protection must be guaranteed regardless of where the power resides
notes: Must be read conjunctively with Art. 32 (dignity) and Art. 42 (non-discrimination); uncertainty on what constitutes 'imminent public health risk' as a legal threshold; the provision requires 'national law' as a precondition — systems lacking this legal basis cannot lawfully apply compulsory measures

article_title: Treatment of travellers
obligation_text_short: States Parties shall treat travellers subject to health measures with respect for their dignity, human rights, and fundamental freedoms; minimize discomfort; take into account gender, sociocultural, ethnic, and religious concerns; and provide adequate food, water, accommodation, clothing, medical treatment, communication, and assistance when quarantined or isolated.
anchoring_reason: Rights protections for detained or isolated persons require domestic legal standards and operational implementation; non-discrimination requirements must be enforceable; material welfare obligations require institutional capacity; communication rights (language access) require regulatory standards
minimum_domestic_requirement: Domestic law or regulation establishing minimum standards for treatment of travellers under health measures; non-discrimination and anti-stigma standards; material welfare obligations (food, water, accommodation, clothing); communication rights including language access; legal oversight mechanism; gender-sensitive and culturally sensitive protocols
rights_implications: Yes – dignity, non-discrimination, humane treatment; right to communication; specific attention to gender, ethnicity, and religion; intersects with ICCPR Art. 9, ECHR Art. 5, CEDAW
federalism_implications: Partial – in federal systems, standards for traveller treatment may vary across subnational jurisdictions; national minimum standards are required to ensure IHR conformity across all locations where health measures are applied
notes: This provision is unusually detailed for an international health instrument and reflects a rights-based approach to public health; CC Workbook does not directly assess traveller treatment standards; implementation requires both legal standards and operational infrastructure (isolation facilities, language services, etc.)

article_title: General rule — no additional health documents in international traffic
obligation_text_short: No health documents other than those provided for under the IHR or in WHO recommendations shall be required in international traffic, except for travellers seeking temporary or permanent residence and for goods/cargo pursuant to applicable international agreements.
anchoring_reason: Requires review and potentially reform of domestic entry documentation requirements; any existing domestic health document requirements not authorized by the IHR or WHO recommendations must be eliminated; contact information forms must conform to Art. 23 standards
minimum_domestic_requirement: Comprehensive audit of existing domestic health document requirements in international traffic; legislative or regulatory reform to eliminate non-conforming requirements; conformity of any contact information or health questionnaire forms with Art. 23 standards
rights_implications: Yes – protects against arbitrary administrative burdens on international movement; non-discrimination in international traffic conditions; right to travel without unjustified health documentation barriers
federalism_implications: Partial – in federal systems, subnational requirements for health documents at subnational PoE may need to be reviewed and harmonized with the national framework
notes: Exceptions are specific and limited; uncertainty on whether existing domestic health document requirements for diseases outside Annexes 6 and 7 conform to IHR standards; traveller questionnaires and contact tracing forms used during outbreaks must be assessed against Art. 23 criteria

article_title: Certificates of vaccination or other prophylaxis
obligation_text_short: Vaccines and prophylaxis administered pursuant to IHR shall conform to Annex 6 (and Annex 7 for specific diseases); a traveller holding a conforming certificate shall not be denied entry on the basis of the disease referenced, unless the competent authority has verifiable evidence that the vaccination was not effective.
anchoring_reason: Requires domestic authorities to recognize IHR-conforming vaccination certificates and to issue conforming certificates; requires a legal prohibition on entry denial to holders of valid certificates; evidence-based exception for ineffective vaccination must be legally defined
minimum_domestic_requirement: National standards for vaccination certificates conforming to Annex 6 format; authority to issue conforming certificates; legal prohibition on entry denial to valid certificate holders; legal definition of the 'evidence of ineffectiveness' exception; alignment with Annex 7 for specific diseases (currently yellow fever)
rights_implications: Yes – right to enter; non-discrimination on the basis of vaccination status where a valid conforming certificate is held; right against arbitrary entry denial
federalism_implications: Partial – vaccination administration may be subnational but certificate formats and acceptance standards must be nationally uniform
notes: Annex 7 currently covers yellow fever; uncertainty on whether domestic vaccination record and certificate systems conform to Annex 6 model certificate format; WHO may update Annexes 6 and 7 — domestic implementation must be kept current

article_title: Maritime Declaration of Health
obligation_text_short: The master of a ship shall complete and deliver a Maritime Declaration of Health conforming to Annex 8 on arrival at the first port in State Party territory, unless the State Party does not require it; the master or ship's surgeon shall supply any health information requested by the competent authority.
obligation_type: Procedural reporting (with State Party discretion to waive)
legal_force: Binding (subject to State Party's decision to require or waive)
anchoring_reason: State Party must have regulatory framework specifying which ships or routes require Maritime Declaration of Health submission; even if waived generally, selective requirements for ships from affected areas require a legal basis; Annex 8 format conformity must be ensured
minimum_domestic_requirement: Regulatory provisions specifying when MDH is required; determination of which ships and routes fall within the requirement or waiver; competent port authority designated to receive declarations; Annex 8-conforming declaration format; informed notification to shipping operators of applicable requirements
rights_implications: No direct rights implications
federalism_implications: No significant federalism implications — maritime port authority is typically a national administrative function
notes: Art. 37(4) gives State Parties discretion to waive MDH submission generally or apply it selectively; uncertainty on whether existing maritime regulatory frameworks in each State already satisfy Art. 37 requirements; shipping operators must be informed of applicable requirements per Art. 37(4)

article_title: Health Part of the Aircraft General Declaration
obligation_text_short: The pilot in command or agent shall complete and deliver the Health Part of the Aircraft General Declaration conforming to Annex 9 on landing at the first airport in State Party territory, unless the State Party does not require it; the pilot or agent shall supply health information requested by the State Party.
obligation_type: Procedural reporting (with State Party discretion to waive)
legal_force: Binding (subject to State Party's decision to require or waive)
anchoring_reason: State Party must have a regulatory framework (civil aviation regulations) specifying when the Health Part of the Aircraft General Declaration is required; Annex 9 format conformity must be ensured; selective requirements for aircraft from affected areas need legal basis
minimum_domestic_requirement: Civil aviation regulations specifying health declaration requirements; Annex 9-conforming declaration format; designated airport authority for collection; informed notification to aircraft operators of applicable requirements
rights_implications: No direct rights implications
federalism_implications: No significant federalism implications — civil aviation regulation is typically a national competence
notes: Art. 38(3) gives discretion to waive or apply selectively; ICAO standards (Annex 9 to the Chicago Convention) may provide an existing legal framework that partially satisfies this requirement; uncertainty on interaction between IHR and ICAO frameworks

article_title: Ship sanitation certificates — issuance and validity
obligation_text_short: Ship Sanitation Control Exemption Certificates and Ship Sanitation Control Certificates shall be valid for a maximum of 6 months with a possible 1-month extension; the competent authority shall issue the appropriate certificate after inspection and satisfactory control measures; certificates shall conform to Annex 3 model.
anchoring_reason: Requires a designated port authority with legal inspection and certification powers; certificates must conform to the Annex 3 model format; issuance is a legal act with international legal effects; the 6-month validity period and conditions for extension must be incorporated in domestic law or regulation
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal authority for ship inspections and certification at designated ports; Annex 3-conforming certificate format and issuance procedure; port authority with enforcement powers if inspection reveals deficiencies; trained and designated inspectors; procedure for 1-month certificate extension where control measures cannot be completed
rights_implications: No direct rights implications
federalism_implications: Partial – port authority jurisdiction may vary between national and subnational levels; legal clarity on which authority has certification competence is required
notes: Closely linked to Art. 20(2); CC Workbook PoE indicators include inspection and certification capacity as a measurable indicator; WHO may certify ports per Art. 20(4); control measures shall be carried out when ship and holds are empty where possible (Art. 39(4))

article_title: Charges for health measures regarding travellers
obligation_text_short: No charge shall be made to travellers (other than those seeking residence) for specified health measures: medical examinations, vaccines on arrival without prior notice, quarantine, certificates, and baggage health measures; a single tariff is required for chargeable measures; charges shall be published at least 10 days in advance; charges shall not distinguish by nationality, domicile, or residence.
anchoring_reason: Requires domestic tariff regulation; the prohibition on charging for specified measures must be legally enforceable; the single tariff requirement, non-discrimination standard, and advance publication obligation are binding procedural requirements needing domestic regulatory implementation
minimum_domestic_requirement: Domestic tariff regulation conforming to Art. 40 provisions; explicit prohibition on fees for IHR-mandated health measures on travellers; single national tariff for chargeable measures; 10-day advance publication requirement; non-discrimination clause by nationality, domicile, or residence; prohibition on denying departure for non-payment (Art. 40(6))
rights_implications: Yes – right to access health measures without financial barriers; non-discrimination in charges; protects against arbitrary fees that deter health-seeking behavior or discriminate against specific nationalities
federalism_implications: Yes – in federal systems, multiple tariff schedules at different PoE may conflict with the 'single tariff' requirement; national standardization of charges is required even if PoE are under subnational or private management
notes: Art. 40(5) allows seeking reimbursement from conveyance operators or insurance sources; Art. 40(6) prohibits denial of departure pending payment; the 'single tariff' requirement is operationally demanding in systems with multiple regulatory bodies

article_title: Charges for health measures on goods, cargo, containers, and conveyances
obligation_text_short: Where charges are made for applying health measures to goods, cargo, containers, conveyances, or postal parcels, there shall be a single tariff not exceeding actual cost; the tariff shall be published at least 10 days in advance; charges shall not discriminate by nationality, flag, registry, or ownership.
anchoring_reason: Requires domestic tariff regulation; single tariff requirement and advance publication obligation must be legally enforced; the non-discrimination standard (no distinction between national and foreign goods or conveyances) must be legally binding
minimum_domestic_requirement: Domestic tariff regulation for health measures applied to goods, cargo, containers, and conveyances; single national tariff not exceeding actual cost; non-discrimination provision with no distinction by flag, registry, or ownership; 10-day advance publication of any tariff or amendment
rights_implications: Partial – non-discrimination in trade and commerce; protection against arbitrary charges affecting international trade; links to WTO obligations
federalism_implications: Yes – in federal systems, multiple subnational tariff schedules may conflict with the single-tariff requirement; national standardization is required
notes: Related to WTO obligations (GATT, SPS Agreement); uncertainty on whether existing port health charges conform to the single-tariff and advance publication requirements; the 'not exceeding actual cost' standard requires transparent cost accounting

article_title: Implementation of health measures — transparency and non-discrimination
obligation_text_short: Health measures taken pursuant to these Regulations shall be initiated and completed without delay, and applied in a transparent and non-discriminatory manner.
obligation_type: General implementation principle and rights safeguard
anchoring_reason: Non-discrimination and transparency requirements must be legally enforceable at both national and subnational levels; domestic law must incorporate these principles as binding standards; provides legal basis for challenging arbitrary or discriminatory application of health measures
minimum_domestic_requirement: Domestic legal standards incorporating non-discrimination and transparency as binding requirements for health measure implementation; administrative review or judicial mechanism for challenging discriminatory application; timeliness standards in domestic law or regulation; public disclosure requirements for health measures
implementation_domain: Rights protection; CC4 – Response; CC1 – Legislation
rights_implications: Yes – non-discrimination as a fundamental right; equal treatment in application of health measures regardless of nationality, origin, or status; transparency as a governance and accountability principle
federalism_implications: Yes – non-discrimination standards must apply consistently across all subnational jurisdictions; national law must set the standard and subnational implementation must conform
notes: This article is a general principle applying across all IHR health measures and operating as a constraint on Art. 43 (additional measures); uncertainty on what 'transparent' requires beyond public disclosure; closely linked to Art. 44 collaboration obligations

article_title: Additional health measures — scientific basis and proportionality
obligation_text_short: States Parties may implement health measures achieving the same or greater health protection than WHO recommendations, not more restrictive of international traffic or more intrusive to persons than reasonably available alternatives; such measures must be based on scientific principles, available scientific evidence, and WHO guidance.
anchoring_reason: Requires domestic legal authority for implementing health measures beyond WHO recommendations; scientific basis requirement implies documented domestic risk assessment; proportionality must be domestically enforceable; compliance with Art. 43(2) criteria must be verifiable and documented
minimum_domestic_requirement: Legal authority for additional public health measures; documented scientific basis requirement in domestic law or regulation; proportionality standard ('not more restrictive than reasonably available alternatives'); mechanism for risk assessment and documentation; compliance review mechanism for Art. 43(2) criteria
implementation_domain: CC4 – Response; CC1 – Legislation; Rights protection
rights_implications: Yes – additional measures may further restrict movement, trade, and liberty; proportionality and scientific basis requirements are the primary rights safeguards; uncertainty on adequate scientific evidence thresholds creates rights risk
federalism_implications: Yes – in federal systems, subnational authorities may independently impose additional health measures; national coordination needed to ensure subnational measures also comply with Art. 43(1)-(2) criteria
notes: Most contested IHR obligation in practice (notably during COVID-19); tension with WTO SPS Agreement obligations; Art. 43(6) requires a 3-month review; uncertainty on what 'available scientific evidence' satisfies the burden; CC Workbook does not separately assess compliance with Art. 43

article_title: Additional health measures — notification to WHO
obligation_text_short: A State Party implementing additional health measures significantly interfering with international traffic shall provide WHO with the public health rationale and relevant scientific information within 48 hours of implementation; WHO shares this information with other States Parties.
anchoring_reason: Requires a designated authority responsible for WHO notification within 48 hours; requires institutional capacity to document and communicate the scientific rationale for additional measures; the obligation is triggered at the national level even if the measure is implemented subnationally
minimum_domestic_requirement: Designated authority for Art. 43 WHO notifications (typically the NFP); documented public health rationale and scientific information for additional measures; 48-hour notification compliance mechanism; clear trigger definition ('significant interference' = refusal or delay over 24 hours per Art. 43(3))
rights_implications: No direct individual rights implications; mechanism is protective of other States' trade and travel interests through WHO accountability
federalism_implications: Yes – in federal systems, subnational additional health measures that significantly interfere with international traffic trigger the national Art. 43(3) notification obligation; a coordination mechanism between subnational and national authorities is required
notes: 'Significant interference' is defined in Art. 43(3) as generally meaning refusal of entry or departure or delay of more than 24 hours; Art. 43(4) authorizes WHO to request reconsideration; Art. 43(7) allows affected States to request bilateral consultation with the implementing State

article_title: Additional health measures — periodic review
obligation_text_short: A State Party implementing additional health measures shall review such measures within 3 months, taking into account WHO advice and the criteria in Art. 43(2) (scientific principles, available evidence, WHO guidance).
anchoring_reason: The 3-month review requires an institutionalized mechanism with formal authority to assess and modify or terminate additional measures; WHO advice must be formally considered and documented; review conclusions must be documented and available for accountability purposes
minimum_domestic_requirement: Statutory or regulatory review mechanism for additional health measures with a 3-month maximum duration before review; designated authority responsible for review; procedure for incorporating and documenting WHO advice; documentation of review conclusions; possibility of termination or modification after review
implementation_domain: CC4 – Response; CC2 – Coordination; CC1 – Legislation
rights_implications: Yes – periodic review protects against indefinite restrictions on freedom of movement and international trade; the review obligation functions as a sunset clause constraint
federalism_implications: Yes – in federal systems, subnational additional measures must also be subject to review within the 3-month period; national authority must ensure review takes place and that WHO advice is considered at the level where the measure is applied
notes: Review does not automatically terminate the measure but requires substantiated justification for continuation; uncertainty on what constitutes adequate incorporation of WHO advice; this provision was frequently disregarded during COVID-19, generating significant IHR compliance concerns

article_title: Collaboration and assistance between States Parties
obligation_text_short: States Parties shall undertake to collaborate with each other, to the extent possible, in: (a) detection and response to events; (b) technical cooperation and logistical support; (c) mobilization of financial resources; and (d) formulation of proposed laws and other legal and administrative provisions for IHR implementation.
anchoring_reason: Art. 44(1)(d) explicitly identifies legislative formulation as an area of cooperation — an express domestic legislative anchoring reference; collaboration agreements may require treaties or MOUs needing domestic legislative authorization or ratification; financial resource mobilization requires domestic budget authority
minimum_domestic_requirement: Domestic authorization for international health cooperation agreements; mechanism for IHR-related technical cooperation; legislative review process for IHR implementation; financial allocation authority for international cooperation activities; legal basis for entering bilateral or regional health agreements
rights_implications: No direct rights implications
federalism_implications: Yes – CC Workbook CC1 (Level 3) includes resources committed to meet IHR requirements beyond the country's borders as an indicator; in federal systems, international agreements may require both federal and subnational authorization
notes: Art. 44(1)(d) explicitly mentions 'formulation of proposed laws and other legal and administrative provisions' — an express domestic legislative anchoring requirement embedded in an inter-state cooperation obligation; Art. 44(3) allows implementation through bilateral, regional, or WHO channels

article_title: Treatment of personal data
obligation_text_short: Health information referring to identified or identifiable persons, collected or received pursuant to the IHR, shall be kept confidential and processed anonymously as required by national law; personal data may be disclosed only when essential for public health risk assessment and management, subject to principles of fair processing, proportionality, accuracy, and limited retention; individuals have a right of access and correction.
anchoring_reason: Confidentiality and personal data protection require a domestic legal framework; the principles of fair processing, proportionality, accuracy, and retention limits must be legally enforceable; the individual right of access and correction (Art. 45(3)) requires domestic legal mechanisms; the exception for public health purposes must be legally bounded
minimum_domestic_requirement: Domestic data protection or public health law incorporating IHR confidentiality requirements; legal basis for processing personal health data in public health emergencies; data minimization, accuracy, and retention limit standards; individual right of access and correction; prohibition on disclosure beyond public health necessity; compatibility with existing national data protection frameworks
implementation_domain: Rights protection; CC1 – Legislation; CC3 – Surveillance
rights_implications: Yes – right to privacy; right to personal data protection; right of individual access to personal health data; right to correction; right to non-disclosure beyond public health necessity; significant rights implications for surveillance systems collecting individual-level data
federalism_implications: Partial – in federal systems, data protection laws may vary across subnational jurisdictions; a national minimum standard consistent with IHR must be established; health data sharing between levels of government must be subject to compatible data protection frameworks
notes: Art. 45(3) grants individuals the right to access their own health data held by WHO — creating a corresponding domestic obligation for States collecting and transmitting this data; uncertainty on whether existing national data protection frameworks (GDPR, HIPAA, equivalents) fully satisfy IHR Art. 45; surveillance systems collecting individual-level data must have a specific legal basis

article_title: Transport and handling of biological substances and diagnostic specimens
obligation_text_short: States Parties shall, subject to national law and relevant international guidelines, facilitate the transport, entry, exit, processing and disposal of biological substances, diagnostic specimens, reagents, and other diagnostic materials for verification and public health response purposes under the IHR.
legal_force: Binding (qualified by 'subject to national law' and 'taking into account relevant international guidelines')
anchoring_reason: Import and export of biological substances is typically subject to regulatory controls; IHR requires facilitation of movement for public health purposes, which may require specific regulatory exceptions, expedited procedures, or inter-agency agreements; domestic law must be reviewed for compatibility with the facilitation obligation
minimum_domestic_requirement: Review of biosafety, customs, and importation regulations to identify barriers to IHR-purpose biological material transport; regulatory pathway for expedited or facilitated movement; defined chain of custody and biosafety standards for IHR-purpose specimens; inter-agency coordination between health, customs, and biosafety authorities
implementation_domain: CC8 – Laboratory; CC4 – Response; CC1 – Legislation
rights_implications: No direct individual rights implications; public safety implications arising from biosafety requirements apply to third parties
federalism_implications: Partial – customs is typically a national competence, but laboratory regulation may be subnational in federal systems; inter-agency coordination must span levels of government
notes: 'Subject to national law' qualifier means existing domestic biosafety and customs law may limit the facilitation obligation within permissible bounds; uncertainty on how to balance facilitation with legitimate biosafety restrictions; WHO collaborating centre networks depend on this provision; linked to IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations and WHO Guidance on Regulations for the Transport of Infectious Substances
